coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of multicolor room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots for information encryption applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

Zhao, Yiming
Xue, Wenfei
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots (RTP CDs) are widely applied in fields due to their unique phosphorescent properties. However, some current synthesis methods for RTP CDs are relatively complex and the RTP CDs often have poor phosphorescence stability. Here, the multicolor RTP CDs with green and yellow was proposed in a one-step method. Using biuret as the precursor, the introduction of different matrices (boric acid and inorganic salts) makes the RTP CDs emitting different phosphorescence colors. Both of the RTP CDs exhibit excellent resistance to both prolonged UV exposure and ambient air. Structural analysis reveals that the hydrogen bonds formed between the RTP CDs and the matrix exhibits good oxygen-blocking and water-resistance properties, resulting in good phosphorescence stability. The carbonyl and nitrogen heterocycles on the biuret are the main contributors to the phosphorescent emission of the CDs. Moreover, the halogen bonds and covalent bonds between the CDs and the matrix also influence the phosphorescence emission wavelength. Additionally, the RTP CDs were successfully applied in information encryption. This study provides an effective strategy for preparing RTP CDs with improved phosphorescence stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorescent carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) g-RTP-CDs TEM images at low multiples; (b) g-RTP-CDs TEM images at high multiples, embedded: HRTEM images of g-RTP-CDs; (c) Particle size distribution of g-RTP-CDs; (d) y-RTP-CDs TEM images at low multiples; (e) y-RTP-CDs TEM images at high multiples, embedded: HRTEM images of y-RTP-CDs; (f) Particle size distribution of y-RTP-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of g-RTP-CDs; (b) FT-IR spectra of y-RTP-CDs.
(a–e) Full XPS and high-resolution XPS B 1s, C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra of g-RTP-CDs; (f–i) Full XPS and high-resolution XPS C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra of y-RTP-CDs.
Phosphorescent emission images of g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs obtained at room temperature when UV lamp (365 nm) irradiation is stopped.

The UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and PL emission spectra of (a) g-RTP-CDs and (e) y-RTP-CDs; Fluorescence excitation dependent spectra of (b) g-RTP-CDs and (f) y-RTP-CDs; Phosphorescent excitation and emission spectra of (c) g-RTP-CDs and (g) y-RTP-CDs; Phosphorescent excitation dependent spectra (d) g-RTP-CDs and (h) y-RTP-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of g-RTP-CDs at room temperature (under 365 nm excitation); (b) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of y-RTP-CDs at room temperature; (c) The phosphor lifetime attenuation map of g-RTP-CDs; (d) The phosphor lifetime attenuation map of y-RTP-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence attenuation spectra of g-RTP-CDs in solid state; (b) Fluorescence attenuation spectra of y-RTP-CDs in solid state; (c) Fluorescence attenuation spectra of g-RTP-CDs in water solution; (d) fluorescence attenuation spectra of y-RTP-CDs in water solution.
(a) Phosphorescence intensity of RTP-CDs at different UV lamp exposure times; (b) Phosphorescence intensity of RTP-CDs at different durations of exposure to air.
Information encryption pattern using a mixture of fluorescent and phosphorescent CDs, (a, c) with the 365 nm UV light on; (b, d) with the 365 nm UV light off.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of multicolor room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots for information encryption applications

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots (RTP CDs) are widely applied in fields due to their unique phosphorescent properties. However, some current synthesis methods for RTP CDs are relatively complex and the RTP CDs often have poor phosphorescence stability. Here, the multicolor RTP CDs with green and yellow was proposed in a one-step method. Using biuret as the precursor, the introduction of different matrices (boric acid and inorganic salts) makes the RTP CDs emitting different phosphorescence colors. Both of the RTP CDs exhibit excellent resistance to both prolonged UV exposure and ambient air. Structural analysis reveals that the hydrogen bonds formed between the RTP CDs and the matrix exhibits good oxygen-blocking and water-resistance properties, resulting in good phosphorescence stability. The carbonyl and nitrogen heterocycles on the biuret are the main contributors to the phosphorescent emission of the CDs. Moreover, the halogen bonds and covalent bonds between the CDs and the matrix also influence the phosphorescence emission wavelength. Additionally, the RTP CDs were successfully applied in information encryption. This study provides an effective strategy for preparing RTP CDs with improved phosphorescence stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots (RTP CDs) are a new type of nanomaterial that has garnered significant attention due to their unique luminescent properties [1–6]. RTP CDs have a unique afterglow phenomenon, which means they can continue to emit light for a period of time even after the light source has been removed. This unique luminescent phenomenon gives RTP CDs immense potential in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting [7–11]. Compared with traditional phosphorescent materials, the methods for RTP CDs are simple, cost-effective, environmentally friendly and biocompatible [12–15]. Moreover, the luminescent properties of RTP CDs can be tuned by adjusting the precursors and matrices [16,17] or changing the synthesis conditions [18–20] or performing surface modifications [21,22], making their applications more flexible and extensive.

Nowadays, the synthesis and performance of RTP CDs are widely studied. There are two main synthesis methods for preparing RTP CDs [12,23], one involves using polymers as precursors [24–26], the other involves dispersing carbon dots in a matrix [27–30]. For instance, Yang et al. [31] synthesized polymer-based carbon dots (P-CDs) with blue-green phosphorescence through a one-step hydrothermal method using polyacrylic acid (PMA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors. Results indicated that amide, imide structures within P-CDs and crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect contribute to RTP generation. Yue et al. [32] employed isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI) as a carbon source to prepare nitrogen-doped CDs via microwave treatment and then dispersed the CDs in a polyurethane (PU) matrix to obtain nitrogen-doped CDs@PU composites which emitting green phosphorescence. Despite recent advancements in RTP CDs, most reported emissions are primarily blue and green and they tend to have poor stability, which significantly limiting practical applications [33–35]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a synthesis strategy for RTP CDs with long lifetimes and excited stability.

In this study, RTP CDs were synthesized through a one-step method. Using biuret as a precursor, green and yellow RTP CDs were successfully synthesized with boric acid and inorganic salts as matrices through high-temperature calcination, respectively. The RTP CDs exhibiting excellent phosphorescence stability. The phosphorescence mechanism was explored through structural and optical characterization. Due to their unique phosphorescent properties and stability, the RTP CDs were successfully applied in information encryption, yielding satisfactory results.
Biuret, Boric acid (BA), KNO3 and NaCl are provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All chemical reagents are used without further purification.

Preparation of green RTP CDs (g-RTP-CDs): 1.0 g of biuret and 0.5 g of boric acid (BA) were thoroughly ground and mixed, then placed into a crucible and transferred to a muffle furnace. The mixture was heated to 350 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min and maintained at this temperature for 4 h.

Preparation of yellow RTP CDs (y-RTP-CDs): 2.5 g of KNO3, 2.5 g of NaCl, and 0.5 g of biuret were thoroughly ground and mixed, then placed into a crucible and transferred to a muffle furnace. The mixture was heated to 350 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min and maintained at this temperature for 3 h.

Information encryption and decryption were carried out on a black polystyrene 96-well plate by utilizing the properties of RTP-CDs. The RTP-CDs emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light excitation and phosphorescence in green or yellow after the 365 nm UV lamp was turned off. The materials were placed in the corresponding wells of the plate according to the designed patterns. The plate was then exposed to 365 nm UV light for imaging, followed by capturing images with a smartphone after turning off the UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of RTP CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to characterize the morphology and size distribution of the RTP CDs. As observed in Fig. 1 a–c, the g-RTP-CDs are uniformly distributed within the matrix. Specifically, the average particle size of g-RTP-CDs is 4.24 nm. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images reveal that the RTP CDs exhibit a few lattice fringes, all with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite carbon [36,37], while the majority display an amorphous structure. As shown in Fig. S1a, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of g-RTP-CDs exhibits characteristic peaks of amorphous carbon, along with characteristic peaks of boric oxide (B2O3) observed at 15.0°, 28.2°, 29.9° and 40.3°[2,38,39]. It indicates that the g-RTP-CDs have an amorphous structure and are uniformly dispersed within the matrix. Furthermore, covalent coupling reactions occurred between the CDs and BA, introducing electron-withdrawing boron atoms and forming boron–carbon bonds. As shown in Fig. 1d–f, y-RTP-CDs exhibit an average particle size of 5.69 nm. The HRTEM images (Fig. 1e) display lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm. As shown in the XRD pattern of y-RTP-CDs (Fig. S1b), the diffraction peaks at 24.1° and 30.5° are associated with the characteristics of KNO3, while the peaks at 27.5°, 31.7° [3], and 45.5° correspond to the features of NaCl [40]. This suggests the formation of a recrystallized salt matrix, within which the CDs are embedded [41].

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were conducted on the RTP CDs to gain a deeper understanding of the surface functional groups and internal chemical composition of phosphorescent materials. For the g-RTP-CDs, the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a) reveals characteristic absorption peaks at 3553 cm−1 and 3369 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups, respectively. Additionally, absorption peaks at 1089 cm−1 and 965 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibrations of BOC and the stretching vibrations of BC bonds. [42,43]These findings suggest that significant dehydration and covalent coupling happen between biuret and boric acid at high temperature. Furthermore, the peaks observed at 1606 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 and 1159 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO/CN, CO and CN bonds, respectively. It is indicated that biuret is dehydrated and carbonated.

The chemical composition of g-RTP-CDs was determined by XPS analysis (Fig. 3 a–e). The results indicate that g-RTP-CDs contain four elements: B, C, N and O, the corresponding atomic percentages are respectively 11.1 %, 12.5 %, 12.6 % and 63.8 % (Fig. 3a). In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the B 1s peaks appear at 193.7 eV, 193.1 eV and 192.3 eV, corresponding to BO, B2O3 and BC bonds, respectively. The C 1s spectrum shows four peaks at 289.3 eV, 286.3 eV, 285.1 eV and 284.4 eV, attributed to CO, CO, CC/CC and CB bonds, respectively. In O 1s spectrum, two peaks at 533.5 eV and 532.4 eV are respectively corresponding to CO and CO bonds [43]. This indicates that BA is dehydrated to boron oxide. Meanwhile, the formation of BC and BOC bonds implies the covalent cross-linking between biuret and boric acid. This heteroatom doping effectively creates a bandgap (ΔEST), facilitating the reverse intersystem crossing (ISC) from the triplet state (T1) to the singlet state (S1) and protecting the triplet excitons from quenching [2]. FT-IR and XPS analyses further indicate that the B and N elements are successfully doped in the CDs, enhancing spin–orbit coupling. This results in the ISC efficiency increasing, as well as new triplet excitons generating and eventually the non-radiative transitions are suppressed. Additionally, the polycrystalline structure of B2O3 serves as a spatial confinement mechanism, effectively preserving the room-temperature phosphorescence emission of g-RTP-CDs. According to results above, the formation of g-RTP-CDs can be illustrated as follows: biuret and boric acid initially undergo dehydration and condensation at high temperature, especially for biuret, which contributes to the large the conjugated sp2 system served as luminescent center. At the same time, the covalent cross-linking occurs between biuret and boric acid to form aggregates. With the temperature increasing, the aggregates are further carbonized to carbon dots. In addition, some boric acid is dehydrated to generate boric oxide (B2O3), which is acted as a matrix to in situ embed the CDs.

In the FT-IR spectral analysis (Fig. 2b), a characteristic absorption peak of y-RTP-CDs is observed at 3454 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups. Additionally, absorption peaks at 1662 cm−1, 1453 cm−1 and 1239 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO/CN, CO and CN bonds, respectively. The peak at 1534 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibrations of NH in the amide bond. These results indicate that a significant dehydration and condensation reaction occurred on the surface of CDs during the calcination process, leading to the formation of abundant CN/CO functional groups, which enhance spin–orbit coupling effects. Moreover, intersystem crossing involving N/O atoms facilitates the generation of triplet states [44]. XPS peak fitting analysis reveals the composition of y-RTP-CDs. The C 1s spectrum shows three characteristic peaks at 288.1 eV, 285.7 eV and 284.8 eV, corresponding to CO, CO/CN and CC/CC, respectively. The O 1s spectrum displays peaks at 531.0 eV, 532.6 eV and 535.8 eV, associated with CO, COC and NO, respectively. The N 1s spectrum shows peaks at 398.8 eV, 403.2 eV and 407.1 eV, corresponding to CN, CN/CNC and NO3 −, respectively (Fig. 3f–i). These results indicate that the y-RTP-CDs surface contains abundant nitrogen and oxygen functional groups, leading to surface defects and the formation of new triplet states. The OH, NH2 and CN functional groups can form hydrogen bonds. In addition, halogen in inorganic salt matrix has the ability to suppress the free rotation and non-radiative transitions of luminescent groups. This stabilization and protection of the T1 level successfully enable RTP emission. Thus, surface functional groups and hydrogen bonding are key factors in achieving RTP in CDs [45,46]. Based on the analyses above, the synthetic mechanism of y-RTP-CDs are proposed. NaCl and KNO3 are easily melted to liquid at 350 °C. Meanwhile, biuret is employed as carbon source to in situ synthesize CDs in the salt liquid. After cooling to room temperature, the CDs are confined in the salt crystal which protects the room temperature phosphorescence of the CDs by inhibiting nonradiative transitions of their triplet states.

Through visual observation, it is found that the obtained materials exhibit blue fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 4 ). Upon turning off the excitation source, green and yellow phosphorescence are observed for these two materials, with visible times of 9 s and 5 s, respectively.

Optical characterization was performed to explore the performance of g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs. From the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 5 a), it can be observed that g-RTP-CDs exhibit a characteristic absorption peak around 240 nm, while y-RTP-CDs show absorption peaks around 240 nm and 300 nm (Fig. 5e). Both materials have an absorption peak at 240 nm attributed to the π–π* transition of the CC groups, with the additional 300 nm peak in y-RTP-CDs attributed to the n–π* transition of the CO/CN bonds [47,48], which might account for the bimodal phosphorescence spectra observed in y-RTP-CDs. Further fluorescence emission studies show that the fluorescence emission of g-RTP-CDs undergoes a red shift with changing excitation wavelengths, demonstrating pronounced excitation dependence. From Fig. 5b and f, it can be observed that the optimal fluorescence emission wavelengths for g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs are approximately 480 nm and 475 nm, respectively, resulting in blue emission under UV light. Additionally, the excitation wavelength of g-RTP-CDs does not overlap with its excitation absorption band and its fluorescence emission spectrum exhibits strong excitation dependence. In contrast, the second absorption band of y-RTP-CDs slightly overlaps with the excitation wavelength, exhibiting emission behavior independent of excitation. In the phosphorescence study, as illustrated in Fig. 5d and h, the phosphorescence emission spectra of both g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs also display excitation dependence. This is different from previously reported room-temperature phosphorescent materials, where excitation-dependent room-temperature phosphorescence is mainly attributed to the presence of multiple triplet states within the materials. These results highlight significant differences in the photoluminescence properties of g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs [49], which arise from their distinct absorption and emission mechanisms.

As shown in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence emission maximum of g-RTP-CDs is at 458 nm, while its phosphorescence emission maximum is at 471 nm, indicating a Stokes shift of 13 nm. For y-RTP-CDs, the fluorescence emission maximum is at 455 nm, and the phosphorescence exhibits two emission peaks, with the highest emission peak at 485 nm. The Stokes shift between the fluorescence and the highest phosphorescence emission peak is 30 nm (Fig. 6b). According to the relationship between wavelength and energy, calculations show that the energy gap between the singlet and triplet states for g-RTP-CDs is approximately 0.07 eV, whereas for y-RTP-CDs, the energy gap is about 0.13 eV. These results indicate that both types of CDs have relatively small singlet–triplet energy gaps, which facilitates effective reverse intersystem crossing [50,51]. The smaller ΔEst values help promote the excitation of singlet states to triplet states, thereby enhancing room-temperature phosphorescence emission. As shown in Fig. 6c–d, the phosphorescence decay spectra of the two types of CDs were fitted using a triple exponential function, resulting in phosphorescence lifetimes of 418.3 ms and 15.6 ms, respectively.

As shown in Table S3, the phosphorescence lifetimes of g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs are 418.3 ms and 156.6 ms, respectively. The lifetime decay curves for both types of RTP-CDs can be fitted using a tri-exponential function. This fitting suggests that the phosphorescence decay is influenced by multiple electron transitions, which are likely induced by the complex chemical structures present on the surface of the CDs, indicating the existence of multiple excited triplet states. The longer phosphorescence lifetime observed for g-RTP-CDs compared to y-RTP-CDs suggests that covalent bonds provide a stronger binding effect than hydrogen bonds. The covalent interactions between the CDs and the surrounding matrix contribute to a more stable triplet state, which modifies the energy level structure of the CDs and leads to extended phosphorescence lifetimes. Furthermore, the absolute phosphorescence quantum yields of the CDs powders are approximately 22 % for g-RTP-CDs and 18 % for y-RTP-CDs. These values are notably higher than those typically observed for non-metallic RTP materials, underscoring the efficient phosphorescent properties of these CDs.

Using biuret as a precursor, g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs were synthesized through molten salt reactions involving boric acid and inorganic salts, respectively. After the UV lamp was turned off, the RTP CDs exhibited green and yellow phosphorescence. Based on the experimental results, the proposed mechanism for the phosphorescent emission of g-RTP-CDs is as follows: during the calcination process, as the temperature rises, dehydration condensation occurs between biuret and boric acid. This reaction leads to the carbonization of biuret and the formation of luminescent CDs. Covalent coupling reactions then occur between the CDs and the boric acid, with some boron atoms forming covalent bonds with carbon atoms. These interactions promote n–σ* transitions within the CDs. Ultimately, the CDs are embedded in situ and constrained within the boric acid matrix. The doping of CDs with boron, oxygen and nitrogen atoms results in a reduced energy gap between the singlet and triplet states, facilitating efficient intersystem crossing [52,53]. The strong constraint from the boric acid matrix, combined with the covalent bonds between the CDs and boric acid, effectively confines the CDs. This confinement suppresses non-radiative transitions and enhances the phosphorescent emission of the CDs. It is obvious that the covalent bonds (B-C, B-O-C) between boric acid and biuret benefit to stabilize the triplet states, reduce the vibration and rotation of functional groups. In addition, the B and N elements can promote π → π ∗ and n → π ∗ transitions and populate triplet states by facilitating the ISC process for the generation of RTP. Moreover, the B2O3 generated by dehydration is served as matrix, since it not only immobilizes the functional groups but also suppresses nonradiative transitions of triplet states of CDs.

The phosphorescent emission of y-RTP-CDs is hypothesized to be influenced by the presence of inorganic salt crystals, which create a rigid and stable environment, effectively isolating y-RTP-CDs from triplet oxygen molecules in the atmosphere. This isolation is crucial in reducing non-radiative deactivation processes, which are typically caused by the interactions between excited states and molecular oxygen [54–56]. As a result, the triplet state of y-RTP-CDs is stabilized, which minimizes non-radiative decay and facilitates efficient intersystem crossing from singlet to triplet states. Moreover, the inorganic salt matrix, which includes Na+ and K+ ions, plays a significant role in the emission process. These ions engage in electrostatic interactions with the surface charges of the CDs. Such interactions contribute to the formation of halogen bonds, which are essential in suppressing non-radiative transitions. These bonds also help prevent aggregation-induced quenching, a phenomenon particles clump together, leading to decreased emission efficiency. Consequently, the formation of halogen bonds within the matrix enhances the overall phosphorescent emission of y-RTP-CDs.

The bandgap energy was calculated using the formula (αhν) n = A(hv-Eg ) [57,58], where α represents the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, Eg denotes the bandgap energy and A is a constant. Analysis of the data presented in Fig. S2a and b reveals that the bandgap energies of g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs are 4.93 eV and 3.34 eV, respectively. This may be attributed to the incorporation of different matrices, which plays a regulatory role in the size of the CDs. As the emission color of CDs shifts towards red, their bandgap continuously decreases. This phenomenon can be attributed to the structural characteristics of the carbon core, which is an amorphous nanocrystal composed of an sp2 conjugated structure. As the size of the carbon core increases, the bandgap width gradually narrows, leading to a redshift in the fluorescence emission peak. The larger the core size of the CDs, the narrower the bandgap, which causes the emission spectrum to shift towards longer wavelengths, thereby affecting the fluorescence properties of the CDs.

In a comparative analysis, it is observed that g-RTP-CDs exhibit a longer phosphorescence lifetime compared to y-RTP-CDs. This extended lifetime is attributed to the stronger covalent bonds present in g-RTP-CDs, which provide more effective stabilization of the excited triplet state. Covalent bonding ensures that the triplet state is held more securely, thereby reducing the likelihood of non-radiative decay and extending the emission duration. In contrast, the electrostatic interactions and halogen bonds in y-RTP-CDs, while beneficial in many respects, do not offer the same level of stabilization. Additionally, these electrostatic interactions between Na+ and K+ ions alter the surface defects of the CDs, which can lead to a redshift in the phosphorescent emission wavelength. This redshift is indicative of changes in the electronic structure of the CDs due to these interactions.

The fluorescence lifetimes of g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs in the solid state are found to be 7.61 ns and 4.17 ns, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 7 a–b. According to Table S4, the fluorescence decay curves for both g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs can be accurately modeled using a two-exponential function. This model includes τ1 and τ2, which correspond to intrinsic and surface-fixed recombination processes, respectively. The comparable proportions of these surface and intrinsic states in both types of CDs suggest that their luminescence mechanisms in the solid state are similar, reflecting similar behavior in their light emission processes.

When these CDs are dispersed in aqueous solutions, there is a notable decrease in fluorescence intensity, and the phosphorescence is entirely lost. Table S5 and Fig. 7c–d show that in liquid environments, the fluorescence lifetimes are reduced to 3.79 ns for g-RTP-CDs and 0.78 ns for y-RTP-CDs. For g-RTP-CDs in solution, the lifetime curve is best fitted by a tri-exponential function, with the contributions of intrinsic, edge, and surface states each approximately 30 %. On the other hand, y-RTP-CDs exhibit a fluorescence lifetime that fits a double-exponential model, with 98 % of the emission arising from the intrinsic state. This indicates that the surface state structure of y-RTP-CDs is notably disrupted upon dissolution in water, with only the intrinsic state contributing to the weak fluorescence observed.

These observations confirm that the phosphorescence of RTP-CDs is closely linked to their rigid structure and the presence of abundant surface defect states within the solid matrix. The breakdown of this structure and the disruption of surface states in aqueous environments result in the loss of phosphorescence, underscoring the importance of structural integrity and surface defects in maintaining the phosphorescent properties of these CDs.

To assess the photostability of g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs, a series of experiments were conducted to investigate both the effects of prolonged ultraviolet (UV) lamp irradiation and exposure to ambient air on the phosphorescence intensity of these materials. Initially, how phosphorescence intensity varied with extended UV lamp irradiation time were investigated. The results, illustrated in Fig. 8 a, demonstrate that the phosphorescence intensity of both g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs increased progressively with longer UV exposure. Notably, the maximum emission wavelength remained stable throughout the irradiation period, indicating that prolonged UV exposure does not affect the wavelength of the emitted phosphorescence. Following the UV exposure experiments, the impact of ambient air exposure on the phosphorescence intensity of the RTP CDs were examined as well. After a one-month exposure to air, the phosphorescence intensity of both g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs was found to retain over 90 % of its original value, as shown in Fig. 8b. This result highlights the remarkable stability of these RTP CDs in atmospheric conditions. The observed stability can be attributed to the hydrogen bond framework established between the RTP CDs and the surrounding matrix. This framework creates a protective environment that enhances resistance to oxygen and moisture, which are common factors contributing to the degradation of phosphorescent materials. By effectively shielding the excited triplet states from deactivation, the hydrogen bond network plays a crucial role in maintaining the phosphorescent emission over extended periods. In summary, the photostability tests reveal that both g-RTP-CDs and y-RTP-CDs exhibit excellent resistance to both prolonged UV exposure and ambient air. The hydrogen bond framework between the RTP-CDs and the matrix is essential for preventing the loss of phosphorescence, thereby confirming the robustness and suitability of these materials for applications requiring long-term stability.

As illustrated in Fig. 9 , a novel security model was developed using powders from CDs that exhibit blue fluorescence emission but lack room temperature phosphorescence, in combination with RTP-CDs powders. The construction of the security model involved arranging these powders according to a specific code to form the numerical pattern “888”. Upon excitation with a UV light source at 365 nm, the pattern “888” was visibly illuminated, effectively masking the actual password needed for decoding. The fluorescence from the CDs allowed for the display of this numerical code under UV light, providing an initial layer of security. However, when the ultraviolet lamp was turned off, the true passwords “274” and “769” became readily visible. This transition from a hidden to a revealed state highlights the practical utility of RTP-CDs in security applications. The RTP-CDs, which retain their phosphorescence after the UV light is removed, provide a means to decode or reveal hidden information in a controlled manner. Furthermore, the use of two different colored RTP-CDs introduces a dual-layer of encryption. Each color corresponds to a distinct piece of encrypted information, necessitating the correct matching of colors to obtain the accurate password. This color-matching requirement adds an additional dimension of complexity to the security model, making unauthorized access more challenging. These findings underscore the substantial potential of RTP-CDs in advanced anti-counterfeiting and information encryption applications. The ability to conceal and reveal information through controlled light exposure and color-based encryption represents a significant advancement in the development of secure and sophisticated encoding systems.

In summary, a one-step high-temperature calcination method for preparing multi-colored RTP-CDs was proposed in this study. Using biuret as a precursor, g-RTP-CDs were synthesized with boric acid serving as the matrix, while y-RTP-CDs were produced using inorganic salts as the matrix. The RTP-CDs exhibit a long phosphorescence lifetime and excellent phosphorescence stability. Their phosphorescence lifetimes are 418.3 ms and 156.6 ms, respectively. The quantum yields reached up to 22 % and 18 %. They were demonstrated sustained stability under prolonged UV irradiation and exposure to air. Characterization techniques, including FT-IR, XPS and XRD were employed to elucidate the composition and structure of the RTP-CDs. Results indicate that the carbonyl groups and nitrogen-containing heterocycles in the biuret derivatives are pivotal to their phosphorescent emission. Furthermore, the formation of halogen and covalent bonds between the CDs and the matrices significantly affects the emission wavelength of the phosphorescence. The interaction between the RTP-CDs and their matrices results in the creation of a dense and rigid environment that enhances the photostability of the phosphorescent materials. This stability is crucial for maintaining their luminous properties over time. The practical application of the RTP CDs in information encryption was demonstrated, showcasing their effectiveness in room-temperature phosphorescent conversion. This research introduces a novel method for preparing efficient and stable RTP materials and provides a theoretical foundation for developing advanced phosphorescent information encryption solutions.

Xuechun Xu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jinyue Liang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xujing Zheng: Formal analysis, Investigation. Jie Shen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Liangliang Lin: Writing – review & editing. Xiting Chen: Data curation. Yiming Zhao: Formal analysis. Wenfei Xue: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We warmly acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52004102, 22078125), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190605).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2025.116320.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
